does: This paper presents ProtoMedAgent, a framework that combines prototype networks with language models to generate interpretable clinical reports while preventing hallucinations. The system operates through a frozen visual-tabular backbone that distills features into discrete semantic memory, then generates reports via an iterative optimization process constrained by neuro-symbolic rules and a Scribe-Critic feedback loop. The approach includes a semantic privacy gate using k-anonymity and ℓ-diversity to limit data disclosure. Evaluated on 4,160 patients, it achieves 91.2% comparison accuracy while maintaining clinical interpretability through case-based reasoning rather than black-box predictions.
connects: This work builds on interpretable prototype networks, which have shown promise for case-based clinical reasoning by directly using training examples to justify predictions. It directly addresses limitations of standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation in medical settings, where LLMs frequently hallucinate rationalizations misaligned with visual evidence. The paper extends prior work on neuro-symbolic AI and zero-shot prompting by enforcing hard logical constraints during generation. It also responds to growing concerns about privacy in clinical AI systems, incorporating formal privacy guarantees into the interpretability pipeline rather than treating privacy as an afterthought.
enables: This work enables clinical AI systems that can simultaneously provide case-based interpretability, prevent harmful hallucinations in medical narratives, and maintain formal privacy guarantees. The strict neuro-symbolic bottleneck approach could inspire more verifiable clinical decision-support systems where outputs are mathematically constrained to align with data. Future work could extend this framework to other high-stakes domains requiring both interpretability and privacy, integrate real-time clinician feedback into the Scribe-Critic loop, and investigate how to relax computational constraints while preserving safety guarantees. The semantic privacy gate design could also inform methods for balancing utility and privacy in other multimodal medical applications.
